coredata.dc:title: Polyethylene glycol-impregnated carbon quantum dots-phenolic phase change composites for highly efficient thermal energy storage
coredata.dc:creator: Mahdavian, Faezeh
Allahbakhsh, Ahmad
Rodrigue, Denis
Bahramian, Ahmad Reza
coredata.dc:description: The development of stable smart thermal energy storage systems is the key to reaching breakthroughs in thermal management technologies based on phase change composites (PCC). In this study, high-performance PCC is developed by impregnating polyethylene glycol (PEG), as a polymeric phase change material (PCM), into the porous structure of modified resorcinol-formaldehyde (RF) hydrogels. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and 3-chlorophenol are introduced into the RF structure during the synthesis process to increase energy storage properties and impregnation efficiency. A sol-gel polymerization method is used to synthesize the hydrogels, followed by the impregnation of liquid PEG600 into the hydrogel structure. The low PEG leakage of optimal PCC consisting of 3-chlorophenol and 0.2 wt% CQDs is related to the presence of surface functional groups in the structure of the modified RF hydrogels resulting in the formation of hydrogen bonding and electrostatic forces. The melting enthalpy of the optimal PCC is 67 % higher than the unmodified RF-based PCC, while the encapsulation efficiency is 92.5 %. Our results confirm that thermal energy storage performance can be improved by 40 % through the addition of CQDs and 3-chlorophenol into the polymeric material. These PCC systems show long-term stability in terms of storage/release cycles and have the potential to be used as thermal regulator systems.

head.ce:title: Polyethylene glycol-impregnated carbon quantum dots-phenolic phase change composites for highly efficient thermal energy storage

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of stable smart thermal energy storage systems is the key to reaching breakthroughs in thermal management technologies based on phase change composites (PCC). In this study, high-performance PCC is developed by impregnating polyethylene glycol (PEG), as a polymeric phase change material (PCM), into the porous structure of modified resorcinol-formaldehyde (RF) hydrogels. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and 3-chlorophenol are introduced into the RF structure during the synthesis process to increase energy storage properties and impregnation efficiency. A sol-gel polymerization method is used to synthesize the hydrogels, followed by the impregnation of liquid PEG600 into the hydrogel structure. The low PEG leakage of optimal PCC consisting of 3-chlorophenol and 0.2 wt% CQDs is related to the presence of surface functional groups in the structure of the modified RF hydrogels resulting in the formation of hydrogen bonding and electrostatic forces. The melting enthalpy of the optimal PCC is 67 % higher than the unmodified RF-based PCC, while the encapsulation efficiency is 92.5 %. Our results confirm that thermal energy storage performance can be improved by 40 % through the addition of CQDs and 3-chlorophenol into the polymeric material. These PCC systems show long-term stability in terms of storage/release cycles and have the potential to be used as thermal regulator systems.

ce:section.ce:para: Studies on energy management systems aim to reduce energy consumption, improve efficiency, and lower greenhouse gas emissions. Phase change materials (PCMs) are vital to thermal energy storage technology as they store thermal energy through solid/liquid, solid/solid, or liquid/vapor phase changes [1,2]. An ideal PCM should possess several key features, such as minimal energy loss, structural stability, negligible volume change during repeated phase changes, high phase change latent heat density, fast response to temperature changes, and the proper phase change temperature range for a specific application [3]. However, since most high-performance PCMs store thermal energy through the solid/liquid phase change, they are limited by shortcomings such as leakage through phase change, low thermal conductivity, and poor structural stability, which restrict the widespread use of PCMs [4].

Polyethylene glycol (PEG), as a polymeric PCM with high flexibility, high energy storage density, and a tunable phase change temperature range that can be controlled by the molecular weight, holds great potential for the development of thermal energy management systems [3,5,6]. However, PEG also shares the same limits of leakage and has relatively low thermal conductivity (0.1−0.5 W/m. K) [7–9]. Encapsulating PCM in a supporting platform is a widely acknowledged possible technique to overcome these disadvantages [4]. In conventional polymer blends, leakage typically exceeds 10 wt%. However, porous form-stable PCC, specifically using aerogels, proves to be an effective solution for addressing leakage concerns. The combination of their nanostructure, the high surface tension of PCM within the porosity, capillary forces, and interactions between the PCM and the porous matrix reduces the leakage to less than 1 wt%. Additionally, polymer aerogel systems exhibit higher impregnation levels than other systems [10].

The development of novel shape-stabilized phase change composites (PCC) has attracted considerable attention by incorporating PCM into metal−organic frameworks [11], wood-based composites [5], carbonaceous materials including porous carbon [12], graphene [13,14], reduced graphene [15], multiwall carbon nanotubes (MWCNT) [16,17], and expanded graphite [18], as well as polymeric composites such as polypropylene (PP) [19], poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA) [20], resorcinol-formaldehyde (RF) [21], polyurethane (PU) [22], and ethylene-propylene-diene monomer (EPDM) [23,24]. But recently, a significant amount of research effort was dedicated to aerogel-based shape-stabilized PCC for potential application in thermal energy management. These studies aimed to explore the different properties of form-stable PCC to gain deeper insights into their potential applications in areas such as building energy management [6], thermal regulation in the electrical industry [10], wearable energy storage devices [4], and solar energy saving [22,25]. For example, Shen et al. [26] showed that a morphological change of a regular framework aerogel to rough hole walls and multiple inner connected paths can provide more capillary force for PCM encapsulation. They also improved the encapsulation efficiency in a PCC based on lignin−modified boron nitride (LBN)/polyimide (PI) hybrid aerogel PEG for thermal energy storage [26].

The encapsulation efficiency and optimal shape stability are controlled by the average pore size in the supporting framework, as well as interfacial bonding between PCM and cell walls, which can also affect phonon scattering in the composite [27,28]. A small average pore size (micropores) restricts the molecular motion of the PCM, while very large pores (macropores) are not efficient in encapsulating the liquid PCM via capillary forces [28,29]. Moreover, the PCM degree of freedom is affected by the pore size as it dictates how the PCM can move within the composite structures. Pores with larger volumes than the molecular length of PCM can facilitate the rotation of PCM molecules, resulting in a high level of storage efficiency and stability [7]. Additionally, porous absorption techniques with aerogels are among the most effective methods to encapsulate and prevent the leakage of liquid PCM as a result of extensive capillary forces, van der Waals force, and hydrogen bonding in their pores [26]. PCM can diffuse into the pores via capillary forces and stay inside by hydrogen bonding functional groups in aerogels. In addition, capillary and surface tension forces prevent the leakage of PCM from the supported matrix and improve the PCM shape stability [30]. Interactions between the filler and matrix offer improved composite properties, but the surface chemistry of both the matrix and filler must be considered to achieve the maximum benefits of chemical covalent bonds [31]. Moreover, oxygen−containing functional groups in aerogels can act as nucleation sites to promote PCM crystallization leading to higher latent heat compared to neat PCM [18]. Overall, the porosity and chemical nature of aerogels require precise control to increase the loading capacity of PCM, optimize the heat transfer mechanism, and ensure efficient thermal energy storage by preserving the fusion latent heat [10,32].

In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a highly promising material, earned a significant reputation in the field of thermal energy management which can change the chemistry and structure of aerogel. This is mainly due to their exceptional surface hydrogen bonding ability, broad operating temperature range, good solubility in several solvents, high surface area, and photoluminescence properties [31]. CQDs are zero-dimensional carbonaceous materials composed of carbon nanoparticles in the core surrounded by surface functional groups that can be easily modified. The abundant polar functionalities features found in CQDs offer an intriguing prospect for their use as nanomaterials in polymer nanocomposites. These polar groups facilitate the uniform and stable dispersion of CQDs within the composite, resulting in a substantial improvement in the mechanical properties and thermal stability of the nanocomposite [33]. The surface activity of CQDs may be significantly reduced by aggregation and calcination. However, a porous structure can address this problem by acting as a host for CQDs [34]. Additionally, the ability of CQDs to facilitate electron and ion transport has attracted researchers in the field of energy storage [34,35]. Consequently, a porous structure can be considered a promising network for CQDs in energy storage applications. Nevertheless, the presence of CQDs can change the morphology of the porous structure. For example, CQDs were found to decrease the surface area of a porous zinc oxide film by collapsing the mesopores and generating micropores leading to a change in viscosity and increasing the final film thickness [36].

It is believed that the supported matrix has the potential to be tailored to promote energy storage efficiency through structural and chemical modifications. To improve the shape stability and heat storage capacity in the shape-stable PCC based on graphene nanoplatelet aerogels, sodium alginate can facilitate effective PEG absorption by supporting matrix through hydrogen bonding [39]. PEG can serve a dual role by functioning as both the PCM as well as the hydrogen bond provider to successfully achieve a high loading capacity and leakage-free graphene aerogel (GA)/PEG PCC [37]. Different types of carbon materials (biochar, activated carbon, carbon nanotubes, and expanded graphite) have varying PCM content and energy storage capacity based on the physicochemical, microstructural, and thermal properties of the composite PCM [38].

The chemical composition and morphology of resorcinol-formaldehyde (RF) porous structure are key advantages to embedded PCM in PCC technologies. Recent studies on RF in PCC showed 85.7 % encapsulation efficiency by using the polymerization reaction of RF resin on the surface of a three-dimensional skeleton of a loofah sponge. In this leakproof PCC, the carbonization process provided more adsorption space for the PCM and prevented melt leakage [39]. Benefiting from the modified morphology of the nanoscale polymer framework, combined with a mesoporous structure, RF aerogel and cetyltrimethylammonium ammonium bromide (CTAB) as the emulsifier exhibited excellent PEG absorption and anti-leakage properties in battery thermal management [21]. CO2 activation can optimize the porous structure of RF-based carbon aerogel/melamine foam to encapsulate PCM efficiency. Moreover, the high porosity of melamine foam contributes to the structural stability of RF aerogels [25]. Despite significant research advancements in the energy storage performance of PCC in the case of RF which mostly focused on the morphology control and RF-based carbon materials, there is still a considerable gap to be bridged toward reaching optimal encapsulation efficiency inside polymer. To resolve this issue, it is necessary to develop the RF skeleton from a chemical point of view to improve its affinity with PCM.

This study investigates the role of CQDs as a PCM loading promoter and anti-shrinkage material in a phenolic−based aerogel to produce a novel form-stable PCC. This is achieved through the design of porous 3D interconnected networks based on resorcinol-formaldehyde and resorcinol-3-chlorophenol-formaldehyde aerogel modified with CQDs to support PEG. The –COOH–modified CQDs are used as they can be effective in encapsulating our system. The strong hydrogen and electrostatic bonding between the aerogel backbone and PEG facilitates the infiltration process and limits leakage. Moreover, as phenolic aerogel production involves complicated and time−consuming drying processes [40], these modified phenolic−based aerogels can eliminate the need for a drying step making the impregnation process more efficient than what was reported in our previous work [41]. In this way, shape−stabilized and high latent heat PCC is achieved for high−performance energy storage purposes in long-term use.

Resorcinol (C6H6O2) (CAS Number 108-46-3) with 99 % purity was purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific (USA). PEG600 (CAS Number 25322-68-3) and 3-chlorophenol (C6H5ClO) (CAS Number 108-43-0) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Germany). Sodium carbonate (Na2CO3) (CAS Number 497-19-8) with a purity of 99.5 % was obtained from Biobasic (Canada). Formaldehyde 37 % (CAS Number 50-00-0) was purchased from VWR International (Belgium). COOH−modified carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was obtained from Nanochemzone (Canada). All the materials were used as received.

Organic resorcinol−formaldehyde (RF) aerogel was produced through the polycondensation of resorcinol (1,3−dihydroxybenzene) with formaldehyde under alkaline conditions. The synthesis of RF aerogel was performed in distilled water using sodium carbonate (Na2CO3) as the catalyst at a molar ratio of R/C = 200 and R/F = 0.2 (Fig. 1 (a)). In the next step, according to Fig. 1(b), the sol−gel polymerization of the solution was carried out in falcon conical tubes under controlled and well-sealed conditions of 85 °C for 24 h. During the gel preparation, the yellowish solution became orange and finally turned dark red.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Resorcinol−formaldehyde−carbon quantum dots (RF-cCQD) and (RF-sCQD) porous gel
ce:section.ce:para: To produce resorcinol-formaldehyde-CQDs compounds, the basic composition and process parameters followed those used for RF. The weight ratio of CQDs to the solid phase (aerogel) was selected as 0.1, 0.2, and 0.3 wt% for the commercial CQDs (cCQDs), while only a single concentration (0.1 wt%) was selected for the synthesized CQDs (sCQDs) due to limited amount of material to make a comparison (The characterization results for sCQDs and related samples are presented in supporting information). The CQDs was added with the other raw materials as shown in Fig. 1(a (1)).

To prepare RC-cCQD, 3-chlorophenol was first dissolved in distilled water in a molar ratio of 3-chlorophenol/resorcinol = 0.15 and then subjected to agitation using a magnetic stirrer at 1000 rpm for 2 h as reported in Fig. 1 (a (2)). Then, 0.0016 mol of resorcinol in RF-cCQD was replaced with this solution, and the remaining steps of the process were carried out in the same manner as those for RF-cCQD.

The impregnation process in which the hydrogels were immersed in PEG600 for 24 h at 75 °C without prior drying is illustrated in Fig. 1(c). The RF-cCQD-PEG materials were identified as RF-cCQDX-PEG, where X is the weight content (wt.%) of CQDs to the solid backbone of aerogel, while the resulting RC-cCQD-PEG samples were coded as RC-cCQDX-PEG. The RF-sCQD0.1-PEG was assigned to the sample produced using the synthesized PEG600-based CQDs.

Following gold coating, morphological analysis was performed on images taken from a Crossbeam 540 FIB scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Carl Zeiss, Germany). The ATR-FTIR spectra were recorded using a Nicolet iS50 Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) equipped with a mercury-cadmium-telluride (MCT) detector. A JEOL JEM-1230 Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) (JEOL, Japan) was used to image the CQD particles. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to record survey and high-resolution spectra to analyze the surface elemental composition using a PHI 5600-ci spectrometer (Physical Electronics, Eden Prairie, USA) with a standard aluminum X-ray source. Solid-state nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra were recorded on a Bruker Avance 400 MHz system (Bruker Biospin, Canada) with a 4 mm mass probe. The samples were carefully packed in a 4 mm rotor and spun at 12 kHz at a magic angle. Proton spectra were recorded with a single pulse experiment. Carbon spectra were acquired with proton cross polarization optimized with Adamantane (13C). After subjecting the samples to 120 °C under vacuum for 24 h, the nitrogen (N2) absorption-desorption isotherms were obtained on a Gemini VII surface area analyzer (Micromeritics Instrument Corporation, USA) to provide information about the textural parameters of the aerogels. To determine the phase transition temperatures and melting/crystallization enthalpies, differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) analysis was conducted with a single heating and cooling cycle using a Discovery DSC 25 (TA Instruments, USA), in an aluminum pan with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 from −40 °C to 100 °C under nitrogen. c,enPCM are the crystallization enthalpies of PCM and encapsulated PCM, respectively. w refers to the relative weight fraction of PCM in PCC.

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed on a Q5000 IR (TA Instruments, USA) with a heating ramp of 10 °C/min from 35 °C to 850 °C under a nitrogen atmosphere. The liquid PCM leakage test was performed on a heating stage of 100 °C for 1 h and verified by subjecting it to 35 heating/cooling cycles (0 °C–80 °C). The mass loss calculation and visual inspection were performed following the initial weight measurement and placing the PCC samples on filter papers. Upon cooling the samples, the mass of each cycle was determined and subsequently transferred onto a fresh filter paper. The PCC samples were placed on a hot plate set at 80 °C to detect the top surface temperature of cylindrical samples with height and diameter of 13.6 ± 0.1 mm and 12.8 ± 0.1 mm, respectively, and capture the thermal images every 10 s, simultaneously using a thermocouple and thermal infrared camera (FLIR system ThermoVision, Danderyd, Sweden). The thermal diffusivity values were calculated using the hot-disk method as reported previously [45]. The durability of optimized PCC was assessed following 50, 100, and 150 heating/cooling cycles from 0 °C to 80 °C, and then post-treatment samples were analyzed using TGA and DSC techniques.

FTIR results were used to study the surface chemistry of cCQDs, as shown in Fig. 2 (a). The spectrum of the sample suggests the aromatic ring formation by the peaks at 1634. In addition, the bands around 2900 cm−1 were assigned to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of –CH groups on the CQDs [46]. The FTIR analysis revealed the presence of carboxyl functional groups at 1715 cm−1, CH3 at 1402, C–OH at 1287 cm−1, and C–O at 1221 in the cCQDs structures [47,48]. The observations based on the intensity of υC
O band confirm that the cCQDs is a well-modified form of CQDs with –COOH functional groups which is favorable to use in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical analysis of RF-cCQD and RC-cCQD aerogels
ce:section.ce:para: The chemical structure of RF aerogel and aerogels containing CQDs were investigated by FTIR as reported in Fig. 2(b) and (c) to confirm that the CQDs participated in the reaction to form in-situ nanocomposites. The stretching frequencies of 1090 cm−1 assigned to C–O–C bonds revealed the formation of etheric bonds between resorcinol rings in the polycondensation process. The reactivity of the phenolic and carboxylic acid groups in CQDs is significantly amplified when they are transformed into sodium salts through the addition of NaOH solution. This conversion effectively increases the nucleophilicity of these functional groups, thereby elevating their overall level of reactivity [33]. In the aerogel containing CQDs, a new peak at 1164 cm−1 is associated with tertiary alcohol. This indicates that OH functional groups corresponding to CQDs have been incorporated into the aerogel structure. This effect is more pronounced in samples containing higher levels of CQDs (0.2 and 0.3 wt%) as shown in Fig. 2(c). Moreover, the CO peak observed in the CQDs spectrum as shown in Fig. 2(a) is absent in the aerogels containing CQDs since it completely reacted.
The chemical composition of aerogels containing 0.2 wt% CQDs was further compared to the reference aerogel (RF) results [41], using XPS (Fig. 2(d)–(j) and Table S3).

As shown in Fig. 2(d), C1 s and O1 s can be observed in the survey scan spectra, while the additional presence of Cl 2p is also revealed in RC-cCQD0.2. The intensity of the binding energy value around 288 eV associated with carbon atoms in C–O bonds of ether linkages (C–O–C) within the backbone structure which are reduced in RF-cCQD0.2 compared to RF-PEG, demonstrated lower crosslinking density (Fig. 2(e) and (f)).

In addition, RC-cCQD0.2 increases again which can be a sign of crosslinking density compensation. The peaks at 533.5 eV, shown in Fig. 2(h) and (i), attributed to C–O–C and C–O–H increased [49]. The C1 s results also show a reduction in the C–O–C signals. This intensity change can be related to C–O–H which confirms the higher amount of oxygen-functional groups. Moreover, the binding energy of 200.76 eV and 202.44 eV in Cl 2p belong to C–Cl bonding in RC-cCQD0.2 (Fig. 2(j)) [50,51].

It is possible to study the effect of CQDs on the aerogel crosslink density in detail based on a relation between the degree of polymerization/condensation and the average number of linkages per resorcinol ring. This can be done via solid-state NMR as presented in Fig. 3 (a). In the polycondensation of polymeric aerogel caused by the crosslinking of methylene (–CH2–) and methylene ether bridge (–CH2–O–CH2–) connecting two adjacent rings, several resonance peaks are observed between 0 and 160 ppm [52]. The peaks located at 150 and 117 ppm are assigned to aromatic rings directly connected to –OH and aromatic rings that are not directly connected to –OH, respectively. The carbons present in methyl ether groups are responsible for the peak at 29 ppm, and the normalized peak is directly related to the amount of linking per resorcinol ring, also known as the crosslink density of the aerogel. According to Fig. 3(b) and Table S2, the addition of CQDs leads to lower crosslink density. However, the presence of 3-chlorophenol increases the crosslink density despite using the same amount of CQDs.

Textural properties of the aerogels using N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm are shown in Fig. 3 (c) and (d) and Fig. S4, while the values are summarized in Table S4. RF exhibits a Type-V adsorption isotherm in Fig. 3(c), indicating a micro and mesoporous structure as reported in our previous work [41]. With the incorporation of CQDs, the adsorption-desorption isotherm behavior of the samples changed into a Type-IV, suggesting the prevalence of mesopores in the sample texture (Fig. 3(d)). This finding aligns with higher mesopores volume in the BJH analysis of these samples as shown in Fig. S5(a)–(d).

It was further observed that aerogels containing 0.2 wt% CQDs reached a pore diameter of 43 nm, which confirms the formation of mesoporous structures (Table S4). It is possible that CQDs can reduce the frequency of active reaction sites and the interaction between them, thereby allowing for higher pore diameters and particle sizes. The surface area of the samples decreased with CQDs due to the increasing particle size of the secondary nanoparticles. The presence of numerous surface functional groups on the RC-cCQDX porous structures affects these samples as depicted in Fig. S4 and Table S4, making it challenging to provide a precise explanation of the results [47].

SEM was also used to investigate the effect of CQDs on the morphology, structural changes, and nanoparticle sizes, as shown in Fig. 4 and Fig. S6. It is clear from Fig. 4(a) that the structure of the RF aerogel is colloidal in nature with an average secondary particle size of 22 nm. Colloidal structures with larger pore sizes were formed in RF-cCQD0.1, RC-cCQD0.1, and RC-cCQD0.2 (Fig. 4(b)–(d)). It appears that the structure becomes less compact with increasing CQDs content. A possible explanation is the CQDs reaction with the phenolic ring inhibiting the chemical active site, as well as nucleation reactions resulting in fewer particles being formed while particle size increased. These findings are consistent with the NMR results (Fig. 3(b)) where higher CQDs contents led to lower crosslink density and larger particle sizes. In the case of 3-chlorophenol, Cl can participate in the reaction with CQDs, so the number of active sites will be compensated and the crosslinking density will improve generating more condensed microstructures for the same CQDs content.

A wide range of density is reported for dried hydrogels, ranging from 0.16 to 0.23 g/cm3. As summarized in Table 1 , several properties of the samples were compared before and after the infiltration process including density, dimensional changes, and leakage. It is clear from these results that CQDs imposed significant changes in these parameters, which are indicators of structural modifications strongly affecting the energy storage performance.

DSC is the main approach to assess the thermal energy storage ability of PCM. The results obtained for PEG600 and different PCC are reported in Fig. 5 and Table 2 . In PEG600, a phase transformation enthalpy of 117.9 J/g in melting and a bimodal distribution in the crystallization stage is observed. This bimodal behavior of crystallization can be related to heterogeneous nucleation during cooling. As PEG600 crystallizes from the molten state, freezing of the melt surface may weaken the intermolecular interactions causing a transition from a homogenized liquid to a nonuniform crystalline state [53–55]. The DSC plot for RF-PEG (Fig. 5(a)) displays two distinct unimodal peaks: one around 20 °C indicating heat release (exothermic) and the other around 4 °C indicating heat absorption (endothermic). But CQDs addition significantly changes both peaks: despite being broader, these peaks are highly similar to those of neat PEG. CQDs generated a significant increase in the melting and crystallization enthalpies, to such an extent that the latent heat reached its highest value (101.4 J/g) at 0.2 wt% CQDs, which represents a 55 % increase over the sample without CQDs. As a result of using 3-chlorophenol to synthesize the solid backbone of the aerogel, higher melting and crystallization enthalpies are obtained compared to RF-PEG and RF-CQD-PEG (Fig. 5(b)).

The RC-cCQD0.2-PEG has a substantial capacity in terms of storing thermal energy because the latent heat reached the maximum value of 109.1 J/g at 0.2 wt% CQDs, which is 67 % higher than that of the base aerogel and only 7 % less than neat PEG600.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction study between PCM and porous solid backbone
ce:section.ce:para: To significantly improve the energy storage density (measured as the phase change enthalpy) in PCC, it is imperative to increase the PCM content [56]. The results for latent heat in Table 2 show a consistent trend with the impregnation ratio and heat storage efficiency depicted in Fig. 6 (a) and (b). On the one hand, The higher the CQDs concentration, the lower the crosslinking density of the aerogel matrix resulting in easier chain separation and a higher impregnation ratio [45]. On the other hand, the most important factor to improve the PCM content in high-performance PCC (RF-cCQD0.2 and RC-cCQD0.2) is the ability of the solid backbone to absorb PEG by hydrogen bonding interaction between the –OH functional groups of PEG and the aerogel with the electrostatic forces between Cl (3-chlorophenol) and –OH (PEG).

The aerogel skeleton is functionally rich and improves the absorption of PCM, which not only prevents shrinkage but also allows for volume expansion. Following PEG impregnation, the RF-cCQD0.1 framework remained intact as shown in Fig. 6(c). In these pictures, no macropores or mesopores are visible and no gaps exist between PEG and RF-cCQD0.1 indicating excellent compatibility and adhesion between the aerogel and PEG. Furthermore, the continuous structure confirms that the PCM is well diffused and the pores are fully occupied. The most likely scenario for this interaction could be hydrogen bonding between the OH functional groups in the aerogel backbone and those in PEG.

FTIR was used to indirectly study the possibility of hydrogen bonding between PEG and the backbone of the supported material in PCC as shown in Fig. 6(d). In PCC, as compared to free PEG, the wide peaks at 3450 cm−1 and the presence of a band at 1642 cm−1 provide evidence for this interaction. These findings along, with the absence of free hydroxyl groups beyond 3500 cm−1, indicate extensive hydrogen bonding and affinity within the system [57].

The interaction of PEG molecules with CQDs relies on the chemical bonds of C–O–C/C–O–H [58]. So, when CQDs are added, more OH groups are present and the interaction between the aerogel skeleton and PCM will increase, providing a good structure to facilitate the PCM diffusivity. This is why PEG absorption will be lower when using sCQD. This can be attributed to two phenomena. Firstly, the peak at 1162 cm−1, which is the indication of OH, is not formed in Fig. S2(b). Secondly, morphological changes are responsible for this lower impregnation ratio and shrinkage. So, COOH functionalized CQD is prepared by a suitable chemical and structural platform for PEG to be absorbed and stored.

Due to an increase in C–O–H functional groups which was previously proved in FTIR and XPS results, PEG absorption by C–O–H was improved (Fig. 6(e)). In RC-cCQD0.2 due to the higher crosslinking density than RF-cCQD0.2, more C–O–C bonds will form and more PCM will be impregnated. In addition, interactions (electrostatic force) between the Cl of 3-chlorophenol and the OH of PEG can be the most important reason to contribute the impregnation efficiency in the optimized RC-cCQ0.2-PEG PCC (Fig. 6(e)).

In addition, the support matrix character can be beneficial in improving the thermal mobility and crystallization of PCM molecules, thus increasing the heat release efficiency of PCC. According to Table 2, the degree of crystallinity in PCC decreased due to a confinement effect and nucleation inhibition. When using CQDs and 3-chlorophenol, molecular interactions between PCM and aerogel are increased, resulting in higher PCM content which can promote crystallization. Moreover, high PCM loading improves nucleation and the arrangement of PCM with the composite to reach a high level of crystallinity. The high degree of crystallinity indicates a strong interaction between PCM and aerogel [59]. Furthermore, when the PCM content increased to a certain level, some of the PCM molecules were located outside of the nanosized pores and became free, so the latent heat can be easily distinguished via DSC although a higher PCM content in the composites resulted in higher fractions of the material in a crystalline phase and thus higher enthalpy [60].

This phenomenon can also be explained by the melting temperature and crystallization kinetics. The melting temperature decreased in encapsulated PCM leading to slower nucleation and growth rates which can result in a lower degree of crystallinity. When molecular interactions are strengthened within a material by introducing the CQDs and 3-chlorophenol, leading to an improved degree of crystallinity, the melting temperature typically increases. Stronger intermolecular interactions, such as hydrogen bonding or other attractive forces, can enhance the stability and order of the crystal lattice structure. As a result, more energy is required to break these interactions and transform the material from a solid to a liquid state. This increased energy barrier leads to a higher melting temperature which contributes to a lower extent of supercooling (0.1 °C) for the optimal PCC in RC-CQD0.2-PEG lower than that of the pristine PCM (3.1 °C). The higher supercooling value of PEG can be another reason for the bimodal behavior of the crystallization process.

All the samples showed reduced energy storage performance when the content of CQDs was increased to 0.3 wt%. The system may suffer from CQDs agglomeration and accumulation at high concentrations preventing the impacts of CQDs presence on the reactions. This can reduce the thermal conductivity in the system because of an increase in the content of impurities and defects. The higher the amount is the CQDs, the greater the number of functional groups leading to more inter/intramolecular interactions (see Fig. 6(e)). This will cause a reduction in PCM absorption and consequently, enthalpy and crystallinity will reduce which is more important in the presence of 3-chlorophenol. As a result, the low impregnation ratio provides free space leading to a reduction in crystallinity. Consequently, 0.2 wt% of CQDs can be determined as the optimal value in which the energy storage will have the highest efficiency and the least amount of energy loss (Fig. 6(b)).

Fig. 7 compares the performance of the PCC in terms of heat transfer (Fig. 7(a)) and measurement by a thermal camera (Fig. 7(b)). The temperature-time plots show that the temperature of the top surface increases, reaches a plateau, and then continues to rise again. There is a direct correlation between the thermal diffusivity and the slope of these curves below the PCC melting point. Furthermore, the experimental conditions imply one directional heat transfer, so the PCM completely melts when the surface temperature reaches the melting point [45].

The temperature increase for RF-PEG is faster, and the thermal transport properties decrease with CQDs addition with a minimum value for RC-cCQD0.2-PEG. It was observed that, within 500 s after heating, the temperature of RF-PEG, RF-cCQD0.1-PEG, RF-cCQD0.2, RC-cCQD0.1-PEG, and RC-cCQD0.2-PEG was 42, 38, 34, 32, and 29 °C respectively, while these samples required 252, 315, 401, 402, and 530 s to reach 30 °C. As a result, higher CQDs content in the solid backbone implies more PCM being impregnated into the composites resulting in a lower temperature under the same heating conditions and a longer time to reach 30 °C.

As the heating time was extended, a temperature plateau appeared associated with PCM solid-liquid phase transition which can store energy as latent heat [61]. During PCM melting, the energy is stored and used to induce phase change and prevent the system's temperature from rising, leading to constant temperatures in specific time intervals along with plateau regions. The plateau region was extended (longer time) in response to increased PCM content due to the presence of CQDs. A longer plateau indicates an extended PCM melting region, as well as constant temperature over a longer time [62]. Thus, higher latent heat of fusion led to a lower thermal diffusion rate and increased specific heat capacity.

Fig. 7(a) shows that RF-PEG exhibits a weak capacity for energy adsorption because there is no noticeable melting plateau and phase change during heating. In contrast, the phase transition plateau becomes wider when CQDs are added showing a significant improvement in energy storage capacity. Therefore, the longest performance period, as well as the lower slope before melting temperature along with reduced thermal diffusivity belong to RC-cCQD0.2 due to the higher PCM amount in the aerogel. These findings support the use of RC-cCQD0.2 as an effective material for thermal insulation (system protection) applications.

The area under the temperature-time curves (Fig. 7(a)) shows a smaller value for CQDs-containing systems compared to RF-PEG, confirming the effect of CQDs on energy storage. In comparison with RF-PEG, this area for RC-cCQD0.2-PEG is 59.1 %, and for RF-cCQD0.2-PEG system is 76.3 %. In other words, RC-cCQD0.2-PEG and RF-cCQD0.2-PEG can store 40.9 % and 23.7 % more energy than RF-PEG. Accordingly, the RC-cCQD0.2 has a good heat-releasing capability among these composites, which implies that the stored heat can be transferred to the heating medium more quickly and efficiently [63].

In the pictures captured by the thermal camera (Fig. 7(b)), the adsorption of heat exhibits a similar trend. This can be explained by the fact that with CQDs addition, structural changes in the aerogel occur, and because PCM has a lower thermal conductivity and a higher content, the heat transfer rate is reduced, while the energy storage is significantly improved. This can be supported by the results of thermal diffusivity as reported in Fig. 7(c). It is generally accepted that the lower the thermal diffusivity, the greater the energy storage ability, which is why RC-CQD0.2-PEG exhibits excellent energy absorption properties.

Fig. 7(a) shows that during the heating process of RC-cCQD0.2-PEG600, excessive energy has been absorbed, and the temperature is unable to exceed the specified value of 45 °C. Therefore, based on the results for energy storage efficiency along with leakage tests performed on RC-cCQD0.2-PEG600, it is possible to use the material for thermal regulation and protect electronic devices under conditions where the temperature is not allowed to reach 50 °C during a cycle of 1800 s. Moreover, because of the intrinsic melting point of PEG600 (∼25 °C), the temperature of the PCC after 500 s is within the comfort zone for building energy storage purposes. This material can also be recommended for wearable energy storage devices requiring operation within a specific temperature range based on the human body, and RC-cCQD0.2-PEG can reach 37–38 °C for cycles of 1000 s.

Table 3 compares literature data for the energy storage of PCC. According to the results of this work, PCM is infiltrated in aerogel with minimal reduction in latent heat and high encapsulation efficiency, making this system more efficient for energy storage applications.

Thermal stability is an important design parameter to determine the performance and operational temperature range of PCC. In Fig. 8 the TGA and corresponding derivative (DTG) measurements of aerogels, PCM, and PCC are presented. According to Fig. 8(a), a mass loss of around 100 °C can be seen in the first stage analysis of aerogel materials. This occurrence is attributed to moisture absorption by the dried samples, a consequence of the hydrophilic nature of their backbones [71,72]. By increasing the temperature, as reported in Table S5, RC-cCQD0.2, and RF aerogels show a weight loss of 10 % each at temperatures of 340 °C and 269 °C, respectively. However, RC-cCQD0.2 and RF-cCQD0.2 were expected to decompose at a lower temperature compared to RF due to their lower crosslinking density (Fig. 3). This suggests that the incorporation of CQDs has a positive effect on the thermal stability of aerogels containing CQDs by forming chemical bonds with the structure of the synthesized samples. As a result, CQDs can act as a mass-transport barrier, thus improving the thermal stability of the PCC [64]. Following the impregnation of PEG600 into the hydrogel, the TGA curves significantly changed and became similar to the decomposition curves of pure PEG600 (Fig. 8 (b)).

During the progressive temperature increase, RC-cCQD0.2-PEG starts to decompose leaving a residual weight of 7.6 % at 850 °C caused by pyrolysis losses of 48 % for RC-cCQD0.2 and ∼99 % for pure PEG600. According to Fig. 8(c) and (d) and Table S6, the onset (Tonset) and maximum peak temperature (Tmax) shift to higher values for RF-cCQD0.2-PEG (279 °C and 400 °C) and RC-cCQD0.2-PEG (310 °C and 404 °C) compared to RF-PEG (251 °C and 386 °C). These findings indicate that no decomposition occurs below 300 °C for the RC-CQD0.2-PEG, which is far away from the phase transition temperature range of PCM; i.e. good stability and reliability for the PCC at low-moderate temperatures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Long-term shape stability and thermal performance of PCC
ce:section.ce:para: The shape stability of PCC during the transition from solid to liquid is a critical aspect of its practical application. The leakage experiment is typically performed to determine the ability of samples to retain their shape over time [73,74]. Fig. 9 (a) shows digital photos of PEG600, RF, and RC-cCQD0.2 before and after the cycling leakage test. In addition, leakage amounts of PCC for 35 consecutive cycles of heating/cooling within the temperature range of 0 °C to 80 °C are presented in Fig. 9(b). The PCC samples show exceptional shape stability and exhibit negligible liquid leakage on the filter papers after 20 cycles.

The service life of PCC depends on their thermal stability since this involves their long-term use to meet the requirements of thermal energy storage devices, including stable latent heat, phase transition temperature, and mass loss [74,75]. To trace the energy storage and thermal properties, DSC and TGA analysis were conducted on PCC before and after 50, 100, and 150 melting and crystallization cycles between 0 °C and 80 °C (Fig. 9(c)–(e)). Despite the continuous storage and release of thermal energy over 150 cycles, the DSC curves show a consistent behavior following the cycling process (Fig. 9(c)). Moreover, the melting enthalpy and encapsulation efficiency decreased only by 3.4 % and 3.3 % respectively, due to the excellent encapsulation of PEG600 by the supported matrix (Fig. 9(d)). Finally, the TGA curves shown in Fig. 9(e) confirm the durability and reliability of RC-cCQD0.2 when used for a long time.

It is worth mentioning that, despite the recognized application of CQDs as a promising material in photothermal conversion [58], photocatalytic [76], luminescence [77], and fluorescence [78] materials, this study aimed to benefit from the properties of CQDs in the energy storage area. Therefore, it is recommended for future research to broaden the use of the developed system to other aspects of energy management devices. In the future, it is our intention to investigate the photo-to-thermal energy conversion properties of RF-based aerogels modified with CQDs for applications relating to solar-to-thermal energy harvesting and conversion [47].

In summary, this research proposed high-performance shape-stabilized phase change composites (PCC) using carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and 3-chlorophenol as aerogel backbone modifiers. The resorcinol-formaldehyde (RF) based hydrogels were synthesized via the sol-gel technique. Afterward, PEG600 as PCM was infiltrated into the hydrogel pores and replaced with water in the structure of the supported matrix. Important factors affecting the thermal energy storage of porous shape-stabilized PCC are the morphology and chemical properties of the supported networks. To improve the energy storage and encapsulation efficiency of PCC, the aerogels were infused with carbon quantum dots and 3-chlorophenol. Based on the abundant functional groups and pore sizes of the aerogels at 0.2 wt% CQDs, the mass fraction of PEG600 was increased leading to a latent heat of 101.4 J/g. Moreover, introducing 3-chlorophenol with the CQDs resulted in synergistic improvement of the latent heat up to 109.1 J/g. Therefore, the energy storage performance in RC-cCQD0.2-PEG and RF-cCQD0.2-PEG systems improved by 40.9 % and 23.7 % respectively, compared to RF-PEG. These findings indicate a significant level of long-term thermal energy storage and release capacity in the PCC as a potential candidate in building, electronic device regulators, and wearable energy management applications.
